label: Devlog 08
title: Devlog #08 — Making the Draft More Approachable
alt: Veilkeeper's squad drafting screen showing new unit information cards with portraits, battlefield roles, strengths, weaknesses, and tactical descriptions alongside the squad selection interface.
summary: The squad drafting phase now includes detailed unit information cards, helping new players understand each unit's role before entering battle.

sections.text: One of my goals for the  demo is making sure new players can understand Veilkeeper without needing a tutorial or prior knowledge. That starts before the battle even begins.

The squad drafting phase now includes unit information cards that explain what each unit is designed to do. Instead of selecting from a list of names, players can quickly learn a unit's battlefield role, strengths, weaknesses, and overall tactical purpose before committing to their squad.

For example, the Knight is presented as a durable frontline anchor that excels at absorbing pressure and creating opportunities for the rest of the team, while also clearly communicating its limitations. The goal isn't to overwhelm players with numbers, but to give them enough context to make informed decisions.

This update doesn't change the combat mechanics themselves, but it significantly improves the first-time player experience. Small usability improvements like these make the game feel more approachable and help players focus on learning tactics instead of guessing what each unit does.

As development continues toward Alpha, I'm spending more time refining the player's first impression. Every improvement to the interface helps Veilkeeper feel a little more like a complete game and a little less like a prototype.
Current Progress

items: Added unit information cards to the squad draft screen
Each card displays portrait, role, strengths, weaknesses, and tactical overview
Improved first-time player onboarding before combat begins

label: Art of the Veil 03
title: Art of the Veil #03 — The World Takes Shape
alt: Concept artwork showing the Veilkeeper overlooking the city beneath a glowing purple rift while holding a staff illuminated by an amber crystal.
summary: The first colored concept for Veilkeeper's key art establishes the game's central visual contrast between the warmth of the home shard and the corruption of the rift.

sections.text: This is the first colored concept for Veilkeeper's key art and one of the clearest visual representations of the game's world so far. Rather than focusing on an individual asset, this piece explores the atmosphere, scale, and themes that define the project.

The composition centers on the Veilkeeper overlooking the city while a rift hangs above in the night sky. The image contrasts two opposing forces that run throughout the game: the warm amber light of the home shard and the cold purple energy of the rift. That visual relationship has become a guiding principle for both the game's art direction and its UI.

Although this is still concept art rather than a finished illustration, it establishes many of the ideas that future artwork will build on. The goal isn't to finalize every detail yet, but to answer larger questions about mood, composition, lighting, and the overall identity of Veilkeeper.

As development continues, this piece will evolve into the final key art used across the website, promotional material, and eventually the Steam page. Even in this early state, it serves as an important milestone in defining the look and feel of the world.
What this establishes

items: The visual contrast between the amber home shard and the purple rift
The overall mood and atmosphere of Veilkeeper
An early composition for the game's key art
A foundation for future promotional artwork
A stronger visual identity for the project

label: Devlog 07
title: Devlog #07 — First Playable Loop
alt: The Veilkeeper main menu and victory screen, marking the first complete playable loop from game start to battle completion.
summary: Veilkeeper now has its first complete gameplay loop, allowing the game to be played from the main menu through battle, victory, and back to the start.

sections.text: Veilkeeper has reached an important milestone: for the first time, the game can be played from beginning to end without developer intervention. While the current experience is intentionally small, every major piece of the gameplay flow is now connected into a complete playable loop.

The current flow is simple: Main Menu → Battle → Victory Screen → Restart. Reaching this point means I can begin testing the game the same way a player experiences it rather than launching individual systems in isolation.

Although these screens aren't visually exciting yet, they represent an important architectural milestone. Combat can now begin from the title screen, run to completion, and cleanly return the player to a stable state ready for another playthrough. That foundation will support everything that comes next.

With the overall flow established, development can shift toward expanding the experience instead of simply connecting systems together. Future work will focus on refining combat, improving the user interface, and building the content that turns this prototype into a complete tactical RPG.
Current Progress

items: Main Menu → Battle → Victory Screen → Restart
Foundation established for future content and progression

label: Art of the Veil 02
title: Art of the Veil #02 — Establishing Visual Identity (UI Icons)
alt: A set of pixel art UI icons including a shield, crystal, potions, and a sword, showing the introduction of color and a cohesive visual style
summary: Introduction of color into Veilkeeper’s UI icons, establishing the amber and purple palette and beginning to define a cohesive visual language.

sections.text: Veilkeeper’s visual direction is starting to take shape through its UI elements. This set focuses on core icons—equipment, resources, and consumables—used throughout the game.

Earlier work was primarily grayscale to focus on value and readability. This pass introduces color into the system, specifically along the amber and purple spectrum that defines Veilkeeper’s world. Amber represents stability and the home shard, while purple reflects the instability of the rift.

The goal with this set wasn’t just to produce individual assets, but to begin building a cohesive visual language. Each icon is designed to read clearly at small sizes while still reinforcing material differences such as metal, glass, and crystal. Consistency across these elements is key, as they will appear repeatedly in the UI.

There’s still work to do—particularly in pushing contrast and improving material definition—but this is the first group of assets that feels like it belongs to the same system rather than isolated pieces.
Current Progress

items: Introduced color into UI icon pipeline
Created initial set of icons (equipment, potions, crystal)
Focused on readability at small scale
Beginning to define consistent visual language

label: Devlog 06
title: Tag Validation Layer
alt: Tag validation system catching invalid data entries in development
summary: A startup validation layer prevents silent failures in the data-driven tag system.

sections.text: Veilkeeper’s combat system is heavily data-driven, which makes it flexible—but it also introduces a subtle risk: silent failure.

If a tag is misspelled, nothing breaks. For example, adding a “poison” effect but typing “pooison” on a weapon won’t cause an error—the system simply falls back to a default and the effect never applies. These kinds of issues are easy to miss and can persist unnoticed for long periods.

To address this, I added a validation layer that runs at startup. It checks all tag usage across data files against a centralized registry of valid tags. If an invalid tag is found, the game will fail fast in development builds and log the issue in release builds. This allows errors to be caught early without impacting runtime stability.

The result is a safer data-driven system. Tags can still be extended freely without modifying core code, but mistakes are now surfaced immediately instead of silently degrading behavior. As more systems build on tags, catching these issues early becomes critical.
Current progress

items: Startup validation checks across all data definitions
Debug builds fail on invalid tags
Release builds log issues without interrupting gameplay
Tag system supports safe extension without code changes

label: Art of the Veil 01
title: Art of the Veil #01 — First Crystal Study
alt: Side-by-side comparison of a rough and refined pixel art crystal used as a selection indicator in a tactical RPG
summary: First pixel art study for Veilkeeper, showing the evolution of a selection crystal from a rough initial shape to a more refined and readable form.

sections.text: As development continues, I’ve started learning pixel art alongside building the game. This is the first original asset created specifically for Veilkeeper — a selection crystal used in the combat grid.

The goal wasn’t to make something perfect, but to create something readable and functional that could fit into the game’s visual language. The crystal is designed in grayscale so it can be tinted later based on context, such as player actions, enemy states, or signal strength.

The difference between the two versions highlights a few key improvements: clearer shape definition, better separation of light and shadow, and a stronger overall silhouette. Even small adjustments to shading made a noticeable impact on readability.

This process is part of a larger effort to build a consistent visual identity for Veilkeeper. Early assets like this won’t be final, but they establish direction and make it easier to refine or replace them later with more polished work.
What improved

items: More defined light and shadow planes
Better foundation for future UI elements

sections.text: There’s still a long way to go, but this is the point where the visual side of the game starts to feel real. Future Art of the Veil posts will continue to explore UI elements, assets, and the evolving look of the game.

label: Devlog 05
title: Turn Readability & Unit States
alt: Units dim after acting with clearer turn flow in combat
summary: Combat readability improves with clearer turn flow and units dimming after acting.

sections.text: Combat readability continues to improve as the tactical loop takes shape. Units now visually dim after they’ve acted, making it easier to see which units are still available during a turn.
This also helps clarify enemy turns. With multiple units acting in sequence, the battlefield is easier to follow and the flow of combat is more understandable at a glance.
These changes don’t add new mechanics, but they make the existing systems much clearer. As more interactions are added, readability will become increasingly important.
Current progress
Next up is continuing to improve combat feedback and adding stronger indicators for targeting and damage resolution.

label: Devlog 04
title: Attacks & Unit Death
alt: Units attacking and being defeated in combat prototype
summary: Units can now attack and be defeated. The first full combat interaction is now working.

sections.text: Units can now attack and be defeated on the battlefield. This marks the first complete combat interaction in Veilkeeper’s tactical layer.
With selection and movement already in place, this update adds targeting and damage resolution. Units can now engage enemies, reduce health, and remove defeated units from the board.
This is the first point where the prototype starts to resemble an actual combat loop instead of isolated systems. Movement, positioning, and interaction now work together.
Current progress
Next up is improving feedback and readability as the combat loop continues to take shape.

label: Devlog 03
title: Unit Selection & Movement
alt: Unit selection and grid movement prototype
summary: Units can now be selected and moved across the grid. This establishes the foundation for turn flow, pathing, and future combat interactions.

sections.text: Big early milestone for Veilkeeper: units can now be selected and moved across the grid.
This builds on the cursor and input work from the last update, and it is the first point where the prototype really starts to feel like a tactics game instead of just a board with a highlight.
Current progress
Next up
Interaction on top of movement as I keep building toward the first full combat exchange.

label: Devlog 02
title: Cursor & Grid Interaction
summary: Grid highlighting now supports mouse, keyboard, and controller. Input handling is in place, and the board is starting to feel responsive.

sections.text: Work on Veilkeeper’s tactical cursor and grid interaction is now in place. The board can be navigated using mouse, keyboard, and controller, bringing the prototype closer to feeling like a playable tactics game.

This update focuses on responsiveness and readability. The cursor highlights grid spaces clearly, and input handling is designed to feel consistent regardless of control method. Establishing this early helps ensure that later mechanics build on something intuitive.

Even though the systems are still simple, this stage is important. The cursor is the player’s primary way of interacting with the battlefield, and getting that feeling right makes every future system easier to work with.
Current progress in this stage:
Next up is attaching this interaction to actual units. Selection and movement will build on this foundation and move the prototype closer to a full tactical combat loop.

label: Devlog 01
title: Foundations
summary: Veilkeeper’s tactical layer is taking shape. The current focus is building a clean combat foundation that can support the larger game over time.

sections.text: Development on Veilkeeper is starting with the core tactical layer. The goal is to build the game from the combat foundation outward, making sure each new system has something solid to sit on.

Right now, the focus is on establishing the grid, input handling, and the basic structure of turn-based interactions. This stage is less about visuals and more about making sure the game feels responsive and predictable.

The early prototype is centered around clarity. Movement, positioning, and board state should always be easy to understand at a glance. Even small improvements here make a big difference later when abilities, enemies, and systems begin stacking on top.
Current progress in this stage:

The next step is improving cursor control and interaction across different input methods. Once the board can be navigated cleanly, the focus will shift toward selecting units and moving them across the grid.
